metadata.purpose: Reviewable English sysctl knowledge pack for Qwen proposal grounding before any SLM training or adaptation.
metadata.user_visible_language: Korean is generated by the agent policy layer, not by the SLM.
notes: Entries are paraphrased from official Linux kernel documentation and conservative operations practice.
This file is not an allowlist. The policy layer decides what can be proposed to the user.
High-risk entries should be used as background knowledge and usually require stronger warnings or blocking.

entries.summary: Controls the upper bound for the listen backlog accepted by the kernel.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help during inbound connection bursts when the application also requests a sufficiently large listen backlog.
tradeoffs: Can increase memory used for pending connection queues.
Does not fix an application that accepts connections too slowly.
Effect is limited by the backlog value passed by the listening application.
signals: High SYN_RECV or accept queue pressure.
Inbound connection bursts predicted in the next window.

entries.summary: Controls how many packets may wait in the network device backlog when packets arrive faster than they are processed.
entries.when_it_may_help: May absorb short receive bursts when RX traffic temporarily exceeds packet processing capacity.
tradeoffs: Higher values may increase memory use.
A larger backlog can hide CPU softirq bottlenecks instead of solving them.
Too much queueing can increase latency under overload.
signals: High packet rate with temporary backlog pressure.

entries.summary: Sets the maximum receive socket buffer size available to sockets.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help high-throughput receive-heavy workloads when socket receive buffers are a limiting factor.
tradeoffs: Increases potential per-socket memory use.
Applications may still need to request larger buffers.
It is not a substitute for fixing packet loss or CPU saturation.
signals: Receive-side drops or application receive pressure.

entries.summary: Sets the maximum send socket buffer size available to sockets.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help high-throughput send-heavy workloads when send buffers limit throughput.
tradeoffs: Increases potential per-socket memory use.
Oversized buffers can add queueing delay.
Applications may still need to request larger buffers.
signals: Application send stalls that match buffer pressure.

entries.summary: Sets the default receive socket buffer size for sockets that do not request one.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help applications that rely on defaults and receive sustained high throughput.
tradeoffs: Raises baseline memory use for sockets.
Can affect many applications at once.
Better handled carefully than as a frequent automatic proposal.
signals: Many sockets using default buffer sizes.

entries.summary: Sets the default send socket buffer size for sockets that do not request one.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help applications that rely on defaults and send sustained high throughput.
tradeoffs: Raises baseline memory use for sockets.
Can increase queueing delay if set too high.
Can affect many applications at once.
signals: Many sockets using default buffer sizes.

entries.summary: Controls ancillary data memory available per socket.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter for workloads that use socket control messages heavily.
tradeoffs: Usually not a first-line traffic tuning knob.
Increases possible per-socket memory use.
Needs workload-specific evidence.

entries.summary: Controls busy polling behavior for socket reads on supported devices.
entries.when_it_may_help: May reduce latency for specific low-latency workloads with compatible NIC support.
tradeoffs: Can increase CPU usage significantly.
May waste CPU when traffic is idle or bursty.
Requires hardware and workload validation.
signals: Measured latency bottleneck rather than throughput bottleneck.

entries.summary: Controls busy polling for poll/select on supported sockets.
entries.when_it_may_help: May reduce latency for carefully validated low-latency workloads.
tradeoffs: Can increase CPU usage.
May harm general-purpose multi-tenant workloads.
Requires device and application validation.

entries.summary: Sets the default queuing discipline for network interfaces.
entries.when_it_may_help: Can change latency and fairness behavior when the default queue discipline is inappropriate.
tradeoffs: Changes packet scheduling behavior globally for new qdiscs.
May interact with traffic shaping, congestion control, and interface-specific settings.
Too broad for automatic proposal in this project.
signals: Operator intent to change traffic scheduling policy.

entries.summary: Controls the number of half-open TCP connections that can wait in the SYN backlog.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help absorb inbound TCP connection bursts before they are fully established.
tradeoffs: Uses more memory for pending connection state.
Does not fix application accept bottlenecks.
SYN flood behavior must be considered separately from normal bursts.

entries.summary: Controls SYN cookie behavior used as a defense mechanism when the SYN queue is under attack or pressure.
entries.when_it_may_help: Can help preserve service availability during SYN flood conditions.
tradeoffs: Should not be treated as a normal performance tuning knob.
May bypass or limit parts of normal TCP option negotiation in pressure conditions.
Official documentation warns against using it as a substitute for proper capacity planning.
signals: Security response context rather than routine traffic optimization.

entries.summary: Controls how long sockets can remain in FIN-WAIT-2 state.
entries.when_it_may_help: May reduce lingering closed-connection state when many connections terminate and peers do not close promptly.
tradeoffs: Too low a value can disrupt slow or delayed connection teardown.
Does not address root causes of abnormal peer behavior.
Should be changed cautiously for heterogeneous networks.
signals: Resource pressure from lingering closed connections.

entries.summary: Controls whether TCP TIME-WAIT sockets may be reused under certain conditions.
entries.when_it_may_help: May reduce ephemeral port pressure for high outbound connection churn.
tradeoffs: Changes TCP connection reuse behavior.
Can interact badly with NAT, old paths, or unusual timestamp behavior.
Official guidance treats this as an expert-level setting.

entries.summary: Defines the ephemeral local port range used for outgoing TCP and UDP connections.
entries.when_it_may_help: May reduce ephemeral port exhaustion for many short-lived outbound connections.
tradeoffs: The low endpoint must be greater than or equal to net.ipv4.ip_unprivileged_port_start.
If possible, Linux documentation recommends different parity for the low and high endpoints.
Can conflict with reserved ports or local service port policies.
Firewall and monitoring policies may assume a narrower range.
Should be coordinated with ip_local_reserved_ports.

entries.summary: Reserves local ports so the ephemeral allocator will not use them.
entries.when_it_may_help: Can prevent conflicts between ephemeral outbound ports and known local service ports.
tradeoffs: Shrinks the effective ephemeral port pool.
Requires accurate service and policy knowledge.
Poor fit for automatic traffic-only tuning.

entries.summary: Defines minimum, default, and maximum TCP receive buffer sizes.
entries.when_it_may_help: May improve receive throughput when TCP receive buffering limits large bandwidth-delay paths or receive-heavy workloads.
tradeoffs: Can increase per-connection memory consumption.
Should be considered together with net.core.rmem_max.
Oversizing buffers does not solve packet loss, CPU saturation, or application stalls.

entries.summary: Defines minimum, default, and maximum TCP send buffer sizes.
entries.when_it_may_help: May improve send throughput when TCP send buffering limits high-throughput paths.
tradeoffs: Can increase per-connection memory consumption.
Should be considered together with net.core.wmem_max.
Large buffers can increase queueing delay if not matched to workload.
signals: Application send stalls consistent with buffer pressure.

entries.summary: Controls TCP memory pressure thresholds.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter for systems with many TCP connections under memory pressure.
tradeoffs: Affects global TCP memory management.
Wrong values can destabilize networking under load.
Needs system memory and workload-specific analysis.

entries.summary: Controls TCP receive buffer auto-tuning.
entries.when_it_may_help: Usually should remain enabled so TCP can adapt receive buffers to path conditions.
tradeoffs: Disabling can harm throughput on variable paths.
Changing it broadly affects TCP receive behavior.
Not a good automatic proposal without strong evidence.

entries.summary: Controls idle time before TCP keepalive probes begin.
entries.when_it_may_help: May reclaim dead idle connections sooner for long-lived connection workloads.
tradeoffs: Lower values increase keepalive probe traffic.
Can close connections too aggressively on unstable networks if combined with strict interval and probe settings.
Application-level keepalive behavior may be more relevant.

entries.summary: Controls interval between TCP keepalive probes.
entries.when_it_may_help: May detect dead peers faster after keepalive probing starts.
tradeoffs: Lower values increase probe traffic.
Can be too aggressive for unstable or high-latency paths.

entries.summary: Controls how many keepalive probes are sent before a TCP connection is considered dead.
entries.when_it_may_help: May tune the balance between fast cleanup and tolerance for temporary network loss.
tradeoffs: Fewer probes can close connections during transient network issues.
More probes keep dead connections around longer.

entries.summary: Controls whether TCP returns to slow start after an idle period.
entries.when_it_may_help: Disabling may benefit some bursty long-lived flows after idle periods.
tradeoffs: Can increase burstiness after idle.
May harm fairness or congested links.
Needs path and workload validation.

entries.summary: Selects the TCP congestion control algorithm.
entries.when_it_may_help: Can improve throughput or latency when a chosen algorithm matches the network path and workload.
tradeoffs: Changes global TCP congestion behavior.
Can affect fairness, queueing, latency, and throughput.
Requires operator intent and validation, not automatic proposal.

entries.summary: Controls TCP ECN and Accurate ECN negotiation mode. Current Linux documentation defines values 0-5: no ECN, classic ECN combinations, and AccECN incoming/outgoing combinations.
entries.when_it_may_help: Can reduce loss-based congestion response on ECN-capable paths; AccECN-capable modes can expose more precise congestion feedback when peers and paths support it.
tradeoffs: Requires path and peer support.
Some middleboxes or old paths may behave unexpectedly.
Values 3-5 request Accurate ECN behavior and should be treated as an AccECN deployment choice, not just classic ECN tuning.

entries.summary: Controls TCP Fast Open behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: Can reduce latency for repeated connections when clients, servers, and paths support it.
tradeoffs: Changes connection setup behavior.
Requires application and network path support.
Can interact with middleboxes and security policy.

entries.summary: Controls whether to reset new connections when the listen queue overflows.
entries.when_it_may_help: Can make overload failure more explicit in very specific service designs.
tradeoffs: Changes connection failure behavior for clients.
Can make transient overload more visible as resets.
Usually a sign that application capacity or backlog needs attention.
signals: Explicit operator policy for overload reset behavior.

entries.summary: Controls the maximum number of TIME-WAIT sockets kept by the system.
entries.when_it_may_help: Can limit resource use from extreme TIME-WAIT accumulation.
tradeoffs: Too low can discard TIME-WAIT state and affect TCP safety.
May hide connection churn problems.
Requires careful analysis of port pressure and connection patterns.

entries.summary: Controls early TCP retransmission retry behavior before network-layer intervention.
entries.when_it_may_help: Rarely appropriate for automatic tuning; may be relevant for strict failure-detection policy.
tradeoffs: Can change how TCP handles loss and transient path problems.
Too aggressive settings may break connections on lossy paths.
Requires path reliability analysis.

entries.summary: Controls how long TCP may retry data transmission before aborting a connection.
entries.when_it_may_help: May tune failure detection time in controlled environments.
tradeoffs: Too low can kill connections during temporary congestion or loss.
Too high can keep failed connections and resources around longer.
Not suitable for simple traffic prediction based automation.

entries.summary: Controls retry behavior for orphaned TCP sockets.
entries.when_it_may_help: May affect cleanup of connections no longer owned by an application.
tradeoffs: Can change how long orphaned sockets consume resources.
Too aggressive values can terminate connections unexpectedly.
Requires evidence of orphan socket pressure.
signals: Memory pressure related to orphaned TCP sockets.

entries.summary: Controls the maximum number of orphaned TCP sockets.
entries.when_it_may_help: Can protect memory under severe orphan socket accumulation.
tradeoffs: Too low can terminate connections under load.
May indicate application lifecycle issues.
Global impact makes automatic proposal risky.

entries.summary: Controls whether TCP saves learned metrics for destinations.
entries.when_it_may_help: May be relevant when cached path metrics are harmful for changing network paths.
tradeoffs: Disabling saved metrics can reduce useful path learning.
Can affect startup behavior for repeated connections.
Needs path-specific reasoning.

entries.summary: Controls TCP MTU probing behavior for path MTU issues.
entries.when_it_may_help: Can help with black-hole path MTU discovery problems.
tradeoffs: Path-specific and not a general traffic optimization setting.
Wrong assumptions can reduce performance.
Requires MTU failure evidence.

entries.summary: Controls TCP window scaling support.
entries.when_it_may_help: Usually should remain enabled for high bandwidth-delay product paths.
tradeoffs: Disabling can severely limit throughput on modern networks.
Affects fundamental TCP capability negotiation.
Not appropriate for automatic traffic tuning.

entries.summary: Controls TCP timestamp option behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: TCP timestamps support RTT measurement and protection mechanisms; normally they should not be changed automatically.
tradeoffs: Can affect PAWS and RTT measurement behavior.
Interacts with TIME-WAIT reuse safety.
Protocol-level impact is too broad for automatic proposal.

entries.summary: Controls TCP selective acknowledgment support.
entries.when_it_may_help: Usually should remain enabled because SACK improves loss recovery.
tradeoffs: Disabling can harm performance on lossy paths.
Affects TCP loss recovery behavior globally.
Not suitable for automatic proposal.

entries.summary: Controls minimum receive buffer allocation for UDP sockets.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help UDP receive-heavy workloads under buffer pressure.
tradeoffs: Can increase memory use for UDP sockets.
Workload-specific; TCP signals should not drive this proposal.
Application receive behavior remains important.

entries.summary: Controls minimum send buffer allocation for UDP sockets.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help UDP send-heavy workloads under buffer pressure.
tradeoffs: Can increase memory use for UDP sockets.
Workload-specific; TCP signals should not drive this proposal.
Application send behavior remains important.

entries.summary: Controls UDP memory pressure thresholds.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter for UDP-heavy systems under memory pressure.
tradeoffs: Affects global UDP memory management.
Wrong values can destabilize UDP behavior under load.
Needs system memory and workload-specific validation.

entries.summary: Controls whether the host forwards IPv4 packets.
entries.when_it_may_help: Required for router or gateway roles.
tradeoffs: Changes host routing and security posture.
Can expose traffic forwarding paths unexpectedly.
Not a performance tuning proposal for this system.
signals: Explicit operator intent to run as a router or gateway.

entries.summary: Controls the default IPv4 TTL used for locally generated outgoing packets.
entries.when_it_may_help: May be relevant for constrained routing domains or diagnostic policy, not normal traffic optimization.
tradeoffs: Changing TTL alters reachability assumptions.
Too low can break valid paths.
Too high can make loops persist longer.

entries.summary: Controls global IPv4 Path MTU Discovery behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May be used in narrow namespaces with known PMTU problems or hardened PMTU policy.
tradeoffs: Can break protocols when enabled globally.
Can cause fragmentation or black-hole behavior depending on mode.
Official documentation warns that hardened mode is not for global general use.

entries.summary: Sets the minimum cached Path MTU value for IPv4 destinations.
entries.when_it_may_help: May be adjusted only when the network has known minimum MTU constraints.
tradeoffs: Too high can create fragmentation or delivery failures.
Too low can reduce efficiency.
Should be aligned with interface MTU realities.

entries.summary: Controls whether forwarded IPv4 traffic trusts protocol-learned PMTU information.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter for router workloads with user-space PMTU management.
tradeoffs: Forged PMTU information can cause unwanted fragmentation behavior.
Usually unnecessary for normal hosts.
Should be tied to explicit router policy.

entries.summary: Controls whether kernel-generated IPv4 replies inherit the fwmark of the triggering packet.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help policy-routing setups where RST or ICMP replies must follow marked paths.
tradeoffs: Can change routing of kernel-generated replies.
Requires policy routing knowledge.
Not driven by traffic volume alone.
signals: Misrouted ICMP or TCP reset replies.

entries.summary: Controls IPv4 ECMP nexthop selection and hashing behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May improve multipath distribution in ECMP environments with known flow imbalance.
tradeoffs: Can move flows across nexthops.
Hash behavior is kernel-version dependent.
Wrong policy can harm flow locality or path symmetry.

entries.summary: Controls IPv4 neighbor table garbage collection and unresolved neighbor queue behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help hosts or routers communicating with many directly reachable peers.
tradeoffs: Higher thresholds increase memory usage.
Too low thresholds can evict useful neighbor entries.
Deprecated knobs should not be preferred when newer byte-based controls exist.

entries.summary: Controls IPv4 fragment reassembly memory, timeout, and disorder tolerance.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter for fragmented traffic or MTU-constrained paths.
tradeoffs: Higher memory thresholds can increase fragment reassembly memory use.
Too short timeouts drop valid fragmented packets.
Bad disorder settings can drop valid traffic or risk incorrect reassembly.

entries.summary: Controls INET peer cache size and lifetime behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter on systems communicating with many distinct remote peers.
tradeoffs: Larger caches consume more memory.
Shorter lifetimes can lose useful peer state.
Traffic volume alone is not enough to tune it safely.

entries.summary: Controls IPv4 port allocation, privileged bind range, nonlocal bind, automatic bind reuse, and dynamic address behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May be relevant for high-churn clients, transparent proxying, VRRP-like services, or dynamic address environments.
tradeoffs: Can affect application bind semantics.
Can conflict with security policy around privileged ports.
Some options are documented as expert-only or may break applications.
signals: Explicit nonlocal bind or transparent proxy requirement.

entries.summary: Controls early demultiplexing optimizations for local TCP and UDP sockets.
entries.when_it_may_help: May improve local socket receive path but can add cost for pure routing workloads.
tradeoffs: Disabling may help routing-only workloads.
Changing it can harm local socket workloads.
Requires workload classification.

entries.summary: Controls IPv4 ICMP echo handling, ICMP error rate limiting, source-address behavior, and error extensions.
entries.when_it_may_help: May reduce ICMP response load or align diagnostics with router policy.
tradeoffs: Can hide useful diagnostics.
Rate limits that are too strict can break PMTU or troubleshooting signals.
Echo ignore settings change host observability.

entries.summary: Controls IPv4 multicast group limits, source filter limits, IGMP robustness, and forced IGMP version.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help multicast-heavy workloads with many group memberships or version compatibility needs.
tradeoffs: Higher group limits can increase state and report size.
Forcing IGMP versions can break compatibility.
Requires multicast topology knowledge.

entries.summary: Controls IPv4 per-interface redirect, source-route, reverse-path filtering, fwmark source validation, and martian logging behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May be tuned for routers, asymmetric routing, transparent proxies, or anti-spoofing policy.
tradeoffs: Wrong settings can break asymmetric routing or weaken spoofing defenses.
Redirect and source-route behavior has security impact.
Interface-specific policy must be explicit.

entries.summary: Controls IPv4 ARP reply, announcement, proxy ARP, gratuitous ARP, and ARP cache behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help multi-homed hosts, load balancers, private VLANs, VRRP-like setups, and wireless roaming.
tradeoffs: Wrong ARP behavior can blackhole traffic.
Can leak or suppress reachability unexpectedly.
Requires topology and L2 policy knowledge.
signals: Proxy ARP or private VLAN design.
Wireless roaming with ARP cache issues.

entries.summary: Controls IPv4 forwarding, multicast routing, directed broadcast, local-source acceptance, loopback routing, BOOTP relay, and IPsec policy bypass per interface.
entries.when_it_may_help: May be relevant for router, relay, multicast, NAT, or IPsec appliance roles.
tradeoffs: Can change routing and security posture.
Can expose loopback or local-source traffic unexpectedly.
IPsec bypass settings can violate security policy.

entries.summary: Controls ARP neighbor probe retry behavior for multicast, unicast, application, and resolicitation paths.
entries.when_it_may_help: May tune neighbor resolution behavior on lossy or large L2 networks.
tradeoffs: More probes increase control traffic.
Fewer probes can fail neighbor resolution too early.
Needs L2 loss and peer behavior evidence.

entries.summary: Controls TCP loss recovery, duplicate SACK, early retransmit, RACK/TLP-related behavior, reordering tolerance, and TIME-WAIT assassination protection.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter on lossy or reordering-prone paths after careful packet-level analysis.
tradeoffs: Can alter fundamental TCP loss recovery behavior.
Wrong settings may reduce throughput or reliability.
Usually should not be traffic-volume-driven.
signals: Tail loss or retransmission pattern analysis.

entries.summary: Controls TCP ECN and Accurate ECN negotiation, option sending, beaconing, and fallback behavior. The base tcp_ecn mode supports values 0-5, including AccECN combinations.
entries.when_it_may_help: May reduce loss-based congestion response on ECN-capable paths and may expose more precise AccECN feedback when supported.
tradeoffs: Requires peer and path support.
Middleboxes or old paths may misbehave.
Can change congestion signaling semantics.
AccECN modes and options should be evaluated together rather than as independent traffic-volume tuning.

entries.summary: Controls TCP packetization-layer PMTU probing and minimum MSS behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help with PMTU black holes, tunnels, or unusual low-MTU paths.
tradeoffs: Bad MSS settings can reduce throughput.
Wrong probing behavior can delay recovery or over-probe.
Requires PMTU failure evidence.

entries.summary: Controls TCP pacing, TSO sizing, small queue limits, and unsent write queue thresholds.
entries.when_it_may_help: May tune send-side latency and throughput under high-throughput TCP workloads.
tradeoffs: Can affect latency, burstiness, and throughput.
Too much buffering can increase queueing delay.
Needs send-path measurements.
signals: Latency from buffering or pacing behavior.

entries.summary: Controls TCP SYN retry behavior, listener request migration, and TCP Fast Open behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter for connection setup latency, listener reload behavior, or explicit TFO deployment.
tradeoffs: Can change connection failure timing.
Fast Open affects protocol setup and middlebox compatibility.
Key handling and listener semantics require operator control.

entries.summary: Controls TCP memory pressure, orphan sockets, retry behavior, and socket hash table sizing.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter on systems with very high connection counts or network namespaces.
tradeoffs: Wrong values can destabilize TCP under load.
Hash sizing must be set at the correct lifecycle stage.
Orphan limits can terminate connections.

entries.summary: Controls TCP small-write coalescing, idle slow-start behavior, urgent-pointer compatibility, signed-window workaround, thin-stream timeouts, and ping-pong detection.
entries.when_it_may_help: May tune latency-sensitive or compatibility-sensitive workloads.
tradeoffs: Can change latency-throughput balance.
Legacy options may have no effect.
Compatibility workarounds should be explicit.

entries.summary: Controls duplicate ACK rate limiting, challenge ACK limits, compressed SACK timing, and backlog ACK deferral.
entries.when_it_may_help: May reduce ACK/SACK overhead or tune behavior under malformed or high-loss conditions.
tradeoffs: Overly strict limits can affect protocol feedback.
Compressed SACK behavior changes loss feedback timing.
Requires packet-level evidence.

entries.summary: Controls TCP behavior for VRF/L3 master domains, fwmark inheritance, TOS reflection, and saved destination metrics.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help VRF, policy-routing, or DSCP-reflection designs.
tradeoffs: Can change socket selection across L3 domains.
Can affect route and path metric reuse.
Requires network policy intent.
signals: Stale TCP metrics or DSCP reflection requirement.

entries.summary: Controls TCP Protective Load Balancing behavior for supported congestion control and ECMP environments.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help datacenter ECMP paths with measurable congestion imbalance.
tradeoffs: Can rehash flows and move traffic across paths.
Depends on congestion control and topology.
Not appropriate without ECMP congestion evidence.

entries.summary: Controls TCP minimum RTT windowing, retransmission timeout bounds, and retry limits.
entries.when_it_may_help: May tune failure detection in controlled networks with known latency and loss characteristics.
tradeoffs: Too aggressive timeouts can kill valid connections.
Too relaxed timeouts hold failed connections longer.
Changes affect reliability semantics.

entries.summary: Controls UDP behavior across L3 master domains and UDP socket hash table sizing.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter for VRF-aware services or namespaces with large UDP socket counts.
tradeoffs: Can change socket matching across VRFs.
Hash sizing requires lifecycle planning.
Memory use and NUMA placement can be affected.

entries.summary: Allows globally bound raw sockets to receive packets across L3 master domains when supported.
entries.when_it_may_help: May be relevant for diagnostics or control-plane tools in VRF environments.
tradeoffs: Changes packet visibility across L3 domains.
Can affect diagnostic or security tooling assumptions.
Requires CONFIG_NET_L3_MASTER_DEV.

entries.summary: Controls CIPSO/NetLabel cache behavior and option formatting or validation.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter in labeled networking environments.
tradeoffs: Security labeling behavior can be affected.
Relaxed validation may improve speed but hurt interoperability.
Not a traffic-only tuning target.

entries.summary: Controls the default IPV6_V6ONLY socket behavior for IPv6 sockets.
entries.when_it_may_help: May align application binding semantics with IPv6-only or dual-stack policy.
tradeoffs: Can change whether IPv4-mapped addresses are accepted.
May break applications expecting dual-stack sockets.
Security and service binding policy must be explicit.

entries.summary: Controls IPv6 flow label generation, consistency, state ranges, and reflection behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help ECMP identification or anycast PMTU discovery behavior.
tradeoffs: Can affect flow hashing and ECMP distribution.
Reflection may prevent automatic flow-label changes in some TCP cases.
Requires routing-path awareness.

entries.summary: Controls IPv6 multipath hashing, route notifications, nexthop compatibility mode, and device-down route notification behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May help ECMP route distribution or route-management integration.
tradeoffs: Can alter nexthop distribution and route event visibility.
Compatibility mode affects old route dump consumers.
Route notification changes affect control-plane software.

entries.summary: Controls IPv6 Destination Options and Hop-by-Hop option count and length limits.
entries.when_it_may_help: May harden or relax extension header processing policy.
tradeoffs: Too strict can drop valid extension-header traffic.
Too loose can increase parsing cost or attack surface.
Requires protocol and security policy context.

entries.summary: Defines IPv6 IOAM node identifiers.
entries.when_it_may_help: May be needed for IOAM telemetry deployments.
tradeoffs: Identifier policy is operational, not traffic-volume-driven.
Wrong identifiers can confuse telemetry.
Requires IOAM deployment context.

entries.summary: Controls IPv6 fragment reassembly memory thresholds and timeout.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter for fragmented IPv6 traffic, tunnels, or MTU-constrained paths.
tradeoffs: Higher thresholds increase memory use.
Short timeouts drop fragmented traffic sooner.
Fragment-heavy traffic may indicate MTU or attack conditions.

entries.summary: Enables global IPv6 forwarding and changes host/router behavior across interfaces.
entries.when_it_may_help: Required for IPv6 router roles.
tradeoffs: Changes routing and security posture.
Router Advertisement and Redirect behavior changes with forwarding state.
Should never be inferred from traffic load alone.

entries.summary: Controls IPv6 Router Advertisement acceptance, learned default routes, prefix information, MTU learning, route information, and SLAAC behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May be tuned for hosts, routers that still accept RA, managed networks, or DHCPv6-PD interactions.
tradeoffs: Can add or remove IPv6 addresses and default routes.
Forwarding state changes functional defaults.
Bad RA policy can create loops or wrong routes.
signals: IPv6 default route learned from RA.
Router role with explicit RA acceptance policy.

entries.summary: Controls IPv6 interface forwarding, forced forwarding, redirects, routing extension header acceptance, proxy NDP, fwmark reflection, and nonlocal bind behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May be relevant for routers, VRRP-like services, proxy NDP, policy routing, or anycast services.
tradeoffs: Can change security posture and route acceptance.
Proxy NDP and nonlocal bind require topology awareness.
Wrong forwarding behavior can break host/router semantics.

entries.summary: Controls IPv6 temporary addresses, stable privacy addresses, address-generation mode, retry timing, address limits, and address retention on link down.
entries.when_it_may_help: May align privacy, stable addressing, or interface lifecycle policy.
tradeoffs: Can change source-address selection and privacy posture.
Too many addresses can stress the system.
Stable secrets should be generated and managed carefully.
signals: DAD conflict during stable address generation.

entries.summary: Controls IPv6 Duplicate Address Detection, optimistic address use, Neighbor Discovery notifications, cache clearing, fragmented NDISC handling, and unsolicited Neighbor Advertisement behavior.
entries.when_it_may_help: May tune wireless roaming, first-hop router behavior, DAD conflicts, or NDP hardening.
tradeoffs: Can accept or reject neighbor information differently.
Can affect duplicate address safety.
Security-sensitive NDP behavior should be explicit.
signals: Unsolicited NA or NDP attack concerns.

entries.summary: Controls IPv6 MLD robustness, unsolicited report intervals, and forced MLD version.
entries.when_it_may_help: May matter for IPv6 multicast networks with compatibility or report timing needs.
tradeoffs: Forcing MLD version can break compatibility.
Short intervals increase control traffic.
Requires multicast topology knowledge.

entries.summary: Controls ICMPv6 rate limiting, echo handling, multicast and anycast echo behavior, and anycast error response policy.
entries.when_it_may_help: May reduce ICMPv6 response load or align diagnostics with anycast/multicast policy.
tradeoffs: ICMPv6 is essential for IPv6 operation and PMTU.
Overly strict settings can break diagnostics or path behavior.
Anycast behavior requires service design knowledge.

entries.summary: Disables or enables IPv6 operation on interfaces and can create or remove link-local addresses and routes.
entries.when_it_may_help: May be used only for explicit host policy around IPv6 availability.
tradeoffs: Disabling IPv6 removes addresses and routes on the interface.
Re-enabling creates link-local addresses and may start DAD.
Not a traffic optimization knob.
signals: Explicit IPv6 enablement or disablement policy.
